section.title: Signs of the Coming Siege
section.sectionRationale: Before Ezekiel speaks a single oracle in his own words, God turns him into a walking sermon: a brick city under siege, hundreds of days lying bound on his side, rationed bread and water, and finally a shaved head with its hair burned, struck, and scattered. Each act pictures, in miniature, what is about to happen to Jerusalem in full.
section.thesis: Through humiliating, costly sign acts performed on his own body, Ezekiel dramatizes the certainty and severity of the coming siege and fall of Jerusalem, showing that the city's judgment answers real, prolonged rebellion against a God who had set her above every nation.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the brick city under siege; bearing Israel's and Judah's iniquity by lying bound for hundreds of days; the rationed, defiled bread and water; and the shaved hair divided by fire, sword, and wind, with the oracle of judgment it pictures.

units.label: The brick city under siege
units.note: a sign to the house of Israel

verses.text: “Now you, son of man, take a brick, place it before you, and draw on it the city of Jerusalem.
Then lay siege against it: Construct a siege wall, build a ramp to it, set up camps against it, and place battering rams around it on all sides.
Then take an iron plate and set it up as an iron wall between yourself and the city. Turn your face toward it so that it is under siege, and besiege it. This will be a sign to the house of Israel.
Then lie down on your left side and place the {{avon|iniquity}} of the house of Israel upon yourself. You are to bear their iniquity for the number of days you lie on your side.
For I have assigned to you 390 days, according to the number of years of their iniquity. So you shall bear the iniquity of the house of Israel.
When you have completed these days, lie down again, but on your right side, and bear the iniquity of the house of Judah. I have assigned to you 40 days, a day for each year.
You must turn your face toward the siege of Jerusalem with your arm bared, and prophesy against it.
Now behold, I will tie you up with ropes so you cannot turn from side to side until you have finished the days of your siege.

units.label: Rationed bread and defiled water
units.note: eating and drinking the siege itself

verses.text: But take wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt; put them in a single container and make them into bread for yourself. This is what you are to eat during the 390 days you lie on your side.
You are to weigh out twenty shekels of food to eat each day, and you are to eat it at set times.
You are also to measure out a sixth of a hin of water to drink, and you are to drink it at set times.
And you shall eat the food as you would a barley cake, after you bake it over dried human excrement in the sight of the people.”
Then the LORD said, “This is how the Israelites will eat their defiled bread among the nations to which I will banish them.”
“Ah, Lord GOD,” I said, “I have never defiled myself. From my youth until now I have not eaten anything found dead or mauled by wild beasts. No unclean meat has ever entered my mouth.”
“Look,” He replied, “I will let you use cow dung instead of human excrement, and you may bake your bread over that.”
Then He told me, “Son of man, I am going to cut off the supply of food in Jerusalem. They will anxiously eat bread rationed by weight, and in despair they will drink water by measure.
So they will lack food and water; they will be appalled at the sight of one another wasting away in their iniquity.

units.label: The shaved hair, divided by fire, sword, and wind
units.note: a third burned, a third struck, a third scattered

verses.text: “As for you, son of man, take a sharp sword, use it as a barber's razor, and shave your head and beard. Then take a set of scales and divide the hair.

When the days of the siege have ended, you are to burn up a third of the hair inside the city; you are also to take a third and slash it with the sword all around the city; and you are to scatter a third to the wind. For I will unleash a sword behind them.
But you are to take a few strands of hair and secure them in the folds of your garment.
Again, take a few of these, throw them into the fire, and burn them. From there a fire will spread to the whole house of Israel.
This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘This is Jerusalem, which I have set in the center of the nations, with countries all around her.

But she has rebelled against My ordinances more wickedly than the nations, and against My statutes worse than the countries around her. For her people have rejected My ordinances and have not walked in My statutes.’

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘You have been more insubordinate than the nations around you; you have not walked in My statutes or kept My ordinances, nor have you even conformed to the ordinances of the nations around you.’
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Behold, I Myself am against you, Jerusalem, and I will execute judgments among you in the sight of the nations.
Because of all your abominations, I will do to you what I have never done before and will never do again.
As a result, fathers among you will eat their sons, and sons will eat their fathers. I will execute judgments against you and scatter all your remnant to every wind.’

Therefore as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, because you have defiled My sanctuary with all your detestable idols and abominations, I Myself will withdraw My favor; I will not look upon you with pity, nor will I spare you.

A third of your people will die by plague or be consumed by famine within you, a third will fall by the sword outside your walls, and a third I will scatter to every wind and unleash a sword behind them.
And when My anger is spent and I have vented My wrath against them, I will be appeased. And when I have spent My wrath on them, they will know that I, the LORD, in My zeal have spoken.
I will make you a ruin and a disgrace among the nations around you, in the sight of all who pass by.
So you will be a reproach and a taunt, a warning and a horror to the nations around you, when I execute judgments against you in anger, wrath, and raging fury. I, the LORD, have spoken.
When I shower you with the deadly arrows of famine and destruction that I will send to destroy you, I will intensify the famine against you and cut off your supply of food.
I will send famine and wild beasts against you, and they will leave you childless. Plague and bloodshed will sweep through you, and I will bring a sword against you. I, the LORD, have spoken.”

avon.gloss: Iniquity, guilt, the twisted weight of sin and its punishment together. Ezekiel is told to bear this avon on his own body, day for year, a costly, symbolic picture of a burden the people themselves refuse to feel or confess.

basic.context: Before Ezekiel speaks a single sermon, God makes his very body into a sign. He draws Jerusalem on a clay brick and lays siege to it in miniature. He lies bound on his side for over a year, bearing Israel's and Judah's iniquity day for year. He eats meager, defiled bread and drinks rationed water, exactly as the besieged city will do. Finally he shaves his head and beard and divides the hair three ways, burned, struck, and scattered, a living picture of what will happen to Jerusalem's people when the siege finally breaks the city.
basic.hermeneutics: These are prophetic sign acts, a well-known Old Testament form in which the prophet's own actions dramatize the coming word of God; they are meant to be read as vivid, costly performance rather than literal instructions for later readers to imitate. The point is not the mechanics of numbers and rationing but the certainty and severity of the judgment they portray, and the real rebellion that has made it necessary.
basic.originalLanguages: Ezekiel bears Israel's avon, iniquity, physically, a word that binds together guilt and its punishment as one weight. The oracle explains that Jerusalem has outdone even the pagan nations in rebellion against the ordinances she alone was given, reaching the limit of God's patience.
basic.typology: Ezekiel bearing the iniquity of Israel and Judah on his own body, lying bound and afflicted for their sake, dimly foreshadows the far greater Servant who would actually bear the iniquity of His people, not merely in symbol but in fact, on the cross.
basic.christ: Isaiah's Suffering Servant, who bears our iniquities and is bound and afflicted for us, deepens what Ezekiel's sign act only pictures, and Christ fulfills it fully: He alone truly bears the sin of His people, not for a set number of symbolic days but once for all upon the cross, so that the judgment threatened here need never fall on those who are found in Him.
basic.god: God is patient enough to warn a whole city in advance through years of enacted sign, yet He is also just: Jerusalem's rebellion has exceeded the nations around her, so that the judgment, when it falls, is neither arbitrary nor sudden but the fitting outcome of persistent unfaithfulness.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:4-6 describes the Servant bearing iniquity in a way Ezekiel's sign act only foreshadows. Leviticus 26:27-29 already warned of siege cannibalism as covenant curse, now realized in Ezekiel 5:10. 2 Kings 25 records the literal fall of Jerusalem this section predicts.
basic.oneStory: This is the moment the covenant curses long threatened in the Law become an unavoidable, embodied warning: judgment is coming because rebellion has run its course. Yet even here, a man bearing others' iniquity in his own body points forward to the true Bearer of iniquity, Jesus Christ, whose suffering ends the judgment His people deserved.

context.literary: A tightly linked pair of sign act narratives, chapter 4's siege drama followed by chapter 5's shaved hair and its accompanying oracle, both building to the same conclusion: judgment on Jerusalem is certain, severe, and just.
context.historical: Delivered to exiles in Babylon before Jerusalem's final fall in 586 BC, these acts counter any lingering hope that the city, and the temple, were untouchable. The details, siege ramps, rationed food, cannibalism under siege, match the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28, and were tragically fulfilled in the historical siege recorded in 2 Kings 25.
context.canonical: This section stands in a long line of prophetic sign acts, alongside Isaiah walking barefoot, Jeremiah's yoke, and Hosea's marriage, each dramatizing a coming word of judgment or hope in the prophet's own body and life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To convince exiles who may have hoped for a quick return that Jerusalem's judgment is certain, deserved, and severe, by embedding the message in unforgettable, costly enacted signs rather than words alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, unrepeatable set of prophetic sign acts commanded for Ezekiel alone; it does not prescribe such acts as a normal pattern for believers today. The abiding truth is theological: persistent, covenant-breaking rebellion brings real and severe consequences, and God's warnings, however strange the form, are given in earnest.

debates.question: How should the 390 and 40 day numbers be understood?

views.label: A symbolic period keyed to the years of Israel's and Judah's iniquity
views.case: The text itself explains the numbers, a day for a year, connecting them to a specific duration of national unfaithfulness rather than to a strict, decodable prophetic timetable for later events.

views.label: An approximate span pointing to the length of exile
views.case: Some interpreters connect the total years to the length of exile for the northern and southern kingdoms respectively, though the exact historical reckoning is debated and the text does not spell out every detail needed to fix it precisely.

debates.bearing: Both approaches treat the numbers as meaningful rather than arbitrary, while agreeing that the passage's main force lies in the certainty of judgment for accumulated iniquity, not in constructing a precise chronological calculation.

study.originalLanguages: Ezekiel is made to carry Israel's and Judah's avon, iniquity, on his own body, a word that refuses to separate guilt from its consequence. The oracle in chapter 5 explains why judgment now falls: Jerusalem has outdone even the pagan nations she was set among in rebelling against ordinances given to her alone.

typology.type: Ezekiel bearing Israel's and Judah's iniquity on his own body
typology.fulfillment: A faint foreshadowing of the Suffering Servant who truly bears the iniquity of His people, Isaiah 53:5-6, fulfilled at the cross where Christ actually carries what Ezekiel could only symbolize.

typology.type: Jerusalem set in the center of the nations yet exceeding them in rebellion
typology.fulfillment: A warning that greater privilege brings greater accountability, a principle Jesus applies directly to unrepentant cities in Matthew 11:20-24.

study.christ: Isaiah's Suffering Servant, who was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities, and by whose wounds we are healed, Isaiah 53:5, takes the pattern Ezekiel's sign act only gestures toward and fulfills it completely. Ezekiel lies bound for months symbolizing a burden he does not actually bear in himself; Christ, bound and nailed to a cross, actually bears the real iniquity of His people once for all, so that the judgment this passage describes falling on unrepentant Jerusalem need never fall on those united to Him by faith.
study.god: God is patient enough to spend over a year of enacted warning before the sword ever falls, yet He is unmistakably just: He states plainly that Jerusalem has rebelled worse than the nations around her, so the coming judgment answers real, sustained unfaithfulness rather than divine caprice. Even in wrath God is precise, measuring out judgment by thirds, as He once measured out mercy and covenant blessing.

crossReferences.note: The covenant curse of siege cannibalism, threatened long before, is here declared as coming reality.
The Suffering Servant truly bears iniquity, fulfilling what Ezekiel's sign act only pictures.
The historical siege and fall of Jerusalem that these sign acts foretold.
Jesus warns that greater privilege and revelation bring greater accountability, echoing 5:5-7.

study.oneStory: This is the hinge where warning becomes certainty: a people set at the center of the nations, given more light than any other, have rebelled further than the nations around them, and judgment can no longer be delayed. Yet even as Ezekiel's own body bears a symbolic weight of iniquity, the passage points ahead to the true Bearer of iniquity, Jesus Christ, in whom alone the sword of judgment is finally and fully absorbed.

sermon.title: Divine Judgment and Symbolism: An Analysis of Ezekiel 4-5
sermon.why: Carson works verse by verse through these sign acts, explaining the siege drama, the bound days of bearing iniquity, and the shaved hair, and showing how they dramatize the certainty of coming judgment on Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What did Ezekiel draw on the brick, and what did he then do to it?

options.text: He drew the temple and offered sacrifices on the brick.
options.note: The brick pictures the besieged city, not temple sacrifice.

options.text: He drew the city of Jerusalem and laid symbolic siege against it.
options.note: Ezekiel 4:1-3 describes drawing Jerusalem and building a miniature siege against it.

options.text: He drew a map of the exile route to Babylon.
options.note: The sign is a besieged city, not a travel map.

options.text: Nothing; the brick was left blank as a sign of emptiness.
options.note: The brick is specifically drawn with the city and besieged.

quiz.q: What did lying on his left side and then his right side represent?

options.text: A period of rest before his ministry began.
options.note: The posture was arduous and symbolic, not restful.

options.text: Mourning for his own family alone.
options.note: The bearing of iniquity concerns Israel and Judah, not his family.

options.text: Bearing the iniquity of the house of Israel, then of the house of Judah, day for year.
options.note: Ezekiel 4:4-6 explains both postures this way.

options.text: Two different prophetic visions unrelated to iniquity.
options.note: Both postures are explicitly tied to bearing iniquity.

quiz.q: Why was Ezekiel troubled about baking his bread over human excrement?

options.text: Because he had never defiled himself by eating anything unclean.
options.note: Ezekiel 4:14 records his protest on grounds of ritual purity.

options.text: Because God had not explained the sign to him.
options.note: God had explained it; his concern was personal purity.

options.text: Because he had no fuel available at all.
options.note: His objection was about defilement, not lack of fuel.

options.text: He raised no objection.
options.note: He explicitly protests in verse 14.

quiz.q: What did the three portions of shaved hair in chapter 5 represent?

options.text: Nothing specific; it was purely a fashion statement.
options.note: Each portion is directly interpreted as a form of coming judgment.

options.text: Three different kings who would rule Jerusalem.
options.note: The division pictures the fate of the people, not a king list.

options.text: Three tribes being reunited.
options.note: The sign pictures judgment and scattering, not reunification.

options.text: A third of the people dying by plague and famine, a third by the sword, and a third scattered to the wind.
options.note: Ezekiel 5:2 and 5:12 interpret the divided hair this way.

quiz.q: Why does God say Jerusalem's judgment will be unprecedented?

options.text: Because she is smaller than the surrounding nations.
options.note: Size is not the issue; the issue is the severity of her rebellion.

options.text: Because the surrounding nations pressured God to judge her.
options.note: The judgment flows from Jerusalem's own rebellion, not outside pressure.

options.text: Because she has rebelled against His ordinances more wickedly than the surrounding nations.
options.note: Ezekiel 5:6-9 gives this as the reason for judgment worse than ever done before.

options.text: There is no stated reason for the severity.
options.note: The text explicitly gives Jerusalem's exceptional rebellion as the reason.

questions: Ezekiel's body became a walking sermon long before he preached a word. Where might God be asking you to embody a truth in costly action rather than simply speak about it?

Jerusalem's judgment came because she had been given more light than the nations around her yet rebelled further. Where has God given you unusual privilege or knowledge, and how seriously are you taking the responsibility that comes with it?
Ezekiel bore Israel's iniquity in symbol; Christ bore it in reality. How does resting in what Christ actually accomplished on the cross change the way you carry guilt over your own past sin?
God measured out both judgment and mercy with exact care, by thirds, by years, by weight. How does knowing God's discipline is precise rather than random help you trust Him in a hard season?
The sign acts were humiliating and physically costly for Ezekiel to perform. What is it costing you, practically, to be faithful to what God has asked of you right now?

translationNotes.note: The mixture of wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt into one bread pictures the scarcity of a besieged city, where whatever grain remains must be combined rather than used separately.

Baking bread over dried human excrement, later mercifully changed to cow dung, symbolizes the ritual defilement and desperate conditions Israel would face eating unclean food among the nations of exile.

The threefold division by plague and famine, sword, and scattering to the wind matches almost exactly the historical account of Jerusalem's fall in 2 Kings 25, where famine, the sword, and exile all featured.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 4:1-5:17. No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this passage, and none have been introduced.
